title: The Shadow1
content:
В В В В I dreamed a dream: I dreamt that I espied,
В В В В Upon a stone that was not rolled aside,
В В В В A Shadow sit upon a grave, a Shade,
В В В В As thin, as unsubstantial, as of old
В В В В Came, the Greek poet told,
В В В В To lick the life-blood in the trench Ulysses made,
В В В В As pale, as thin, and said:
В В В В пїЅI am the Resurrection of the Dead.
В В В В The night is past, the morning is at hand,
В В В В And I must in my proper semblance stand,
В В В В Appear brief space and vanish, listen, this is true,
В В В В I am that Jesus whom they slew.пїЅ

В В В В And shadows dim, I dreamed, the dead apostles came,
В В В В And bent their heads for sorrow and for shame,
В В В В Sorrow for their great loss, and shame
В В В В For what they did in that vain name.

В В В В And in long ranges far behind there seemed
В В В В Pale vapoury angel forms; or was it cloud? that kept
В В В В Strange watch; the women also stood beside and wept.
В В В В And Peter spoke the word:
В В В В пїЅO my own Lord,
В В В В What is it we must do?
В В В В Is it then all untrue?
В В В В Did we not see, and hear, and handle Thee,
В В В В Yea, for whole hours
В В В В Upon the Mount in Galilee,
В В В В On the lake shore, and here at Bethany,
В В В В When Thou ascended to Thy God and ours?пїЅ
В В В В And paler still became the distant cloud,
В В В В And at the word the women wept aloud.

В В В В And the Shade answered, пїЅWhat ye say I know not;
В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В But it is true
В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В I am that Jesus whom they slew,
В В В В Whom ye have preached, but in what way I know not.пїЅ
В В В В .В В В В В В В В  .В В В В В В В В  .В В В В В В В В  .В В В В В В В В  .
В В В В And the great World, it chanced, came by that way,
В В В В And stopped, and looked, and spoke to the police,
В В В В And said the thing, for orderпїЅs sake and peace,
В В В В Most certainly must be suppressed, the nuisance cease.
В В В В His wife and daughter must have where to pray,
В В В В And whom to pray to, at the least one day
В В В В In seven, and something sensible to say.

В В В В Whether the fact so many years ago
В В В В Had, or not, happened, how was he to know?
В В В В Yet he had always heard that it was so.
В В В В As for himself, perhaps it was all one;
В В В В And yet he found it not unpleasant, too,
В В В В On Sunday morning in the roomy pew,
В В В В To see the thing with such decorum done.
В В В В As for himself, perhaps it was all one;
В В В В Yet on oneпїЅs death-bed all men always said
В В В В It was a comfortable thing to think upon
В В В В The atonement and the resurrection of the dead.
В В В В So the great World as having said his say,
В В В В Unto his country-house pursued his way.
В В В В And on the grave the Shadow sat all day.
В В В В .В В В В В В В В  .В В В В В В В В  .В В В В В В В В  .В В В В В В В В  .
В В В В And the poor Pope was sure it must be so,
В В В В Else wherefore did the people kiss his toe?
В В В В The subtle Jesuit cardinal shook his head,
В В В В And mildly looked and said,
В В В В It mattered not a jot
В В В В Whether the thing, indeed, were so or not;
В В В В Religion must be kept up, and the Church preserved,
В В В В And for the people this best served..
В В В В And then he turned, and added most demurely,
В В В В пїЅWhatever may befal,
В В В В We Catholics need no evidence at all,
В В В В The holy father is infallible, surely!пїЅ

В В В В And English canons heard,
В В В В And quietly demurred.
В В В В Religion rests on evidence, of course,
В В В В And on inquiry we must put no force.
В В В В Difficulties still, upon whatever ground,
В В В В Are likely, almost certain, to be found.
В В В В The Theist scheme, the Pantheist, one and all,
В В В В Must with, or eпїЅen before, the Christian fall.
В В В В And till the thing were plainer to our eyes,
В В В В To disturb faith was surely most unwise.
В В В В As for the Shade, who trusted such narration?
В В В В Except, of course, in ancient revelation.

В В В В And dignitaries of the Church came by.
В В В В It had been worth to some of them, they said,
В В В В Some hundred thousand pounds a year a head.
В В В В If it fetched so much in the market, truly,
В В В В пїЅTwas not a thing to be given up unduly.
В В В В It had been proved by Butler in one way,
В В В В By Paley better in a later day;
В В В В It had been proved in twenty ways at once,
В В В В By many a doctor plain to many a dunce;
В В В В There was no question but it must be so.
В В В В And the Shade answered, that He did not know;
В В В В He had no reading, and might be deceived,
В В В В But still He was the Christ, as He believed.

В В В В And women, mild and pure,
В В В В Forth from still homes and village schools did pass,
В В В В And asked, if this indeed were thus, alas,
В В В В What should they teach their children and the poor?
В В В В The Shade replied, He could not know,
В В В В But it was truth, the fact was so.
В В В В .В В В В В В В В  .В В В В В В В В  .В В В В В В В В  .В В В В В В В В  .


В В В В .В В В В В В В В  .В В В В В В В В  .В В В В В В В В  .В В В В В В В В  .
В В В В Who had kept all commandments from his youth
В В В В Yet still found one thing lacking, even Truth:
В В В В And the Shade only answered, пїЅGo, make haste,
В В В В Enjoy thy great possessions as thou mayпїЅst.пїЅ
meaning:
This evocative piece by Arthur Hugh Clough represents a masterful exploration of classic. The lines capture a profound emotional resonance, inviting the reader to reflect on the deeper themes of human experience and artistic expression.

In the broader context of Arthur Hugh Clough's bibliography, these specific lines stand out for their clarity and poignancy. Whether you are searching for inspiration or a moment of quiet contemplation, this work offers a timeless perspective that transcends its original era.
